items.q: Why must students distinguish the narrator from the author?
items.correct: The narrator is a constructed voice within the text; the author is the real person who created it. Conflating them misreads irony, unreliable narration and persona.
items.misconception: The narrator's views are always the author's views.
items.random_wrong: The narrator and author are different words for the same thing.
items.why: Students attribute everything a first-person narrator says to the author. This collapses irony — a novel like 'Lolita' cannot work if you take Humbert as Nabokov.

items.q: What is the structure of a Shakespearean sonnet?
items.correct: Fourteen lines in iambic pentameter, structured as three quatrains and a final couplet, rhyming ABAB CDCD EFEF GG, often with a volta near the couplet.
items.misconception: A sonnet is any short love poem.
items.random_wrong: A sonnet must always be exactly ten lines long.
items.why: Students treat sonnet as a vague genre. It is a strict 14-line form with specific rhyme schemes — and sonnets need not be about love (think Hopkins's religious sonnets).

items.correct: A fatal error or flaw in the tragic hero that leads to their downfall — sometimes a character defect, sometimes a mistake in judgement.
items.misconception: Hamartia is the tragic hero's evil personality.
items.random_wrong: Hamartia is the moment the tragic hero dies.
items.why: Tragic heroes are not villains. Hamartia is often a noble quality taken too far (Othello's love, Macbeth's ambition) or a misjudgement — not simple wickedness.

items.q: Which historical figure does Napoleon represent?
items.why: It's Stalin. “Lenin” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is Squealer's role on the farm?
items.why: It's Propagandist. “Soldier” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many Commandments remain at the end?
items.why: Exact answer: 1. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
